title: Hungarian Goulash

overview.description: A rich Hungarian beef stew slowly cooked with onions, sweet paprika and peppers until meltingly tender. Deeply comforting and perfect served with rustic bread.

ingredients: 800g braising beef, cut into large cubes
method: Heat the lard or oil in a heavy casserole over a medium heat.
Add the onions and cook gently for 15–20 minutes until soft, sweet and lightly golden.
Stir in the garlic, then remove the pan from the heat before adding the sweet paprika, smoked paprika and caraway seeds. Stir well to prevent the paprika from burning.
Return the pan to the heat and stir in the tomato purée.
Add the beef and cook until lightly browned on all sides.
Stir in the peppers, chopped tomatoes, beef stock and bay leaves.
Bring to a gentle simmer, cover and cook for about 2½ hours, stirring occasionally, until the beef is fork tender.
Remove the bay leaves and season well with salt and freshly ground black pepper.
Scatter with chopped parsley before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Paprika became Hungary's national spice after peppers arrived from the Americas in the 16th century. Today, Hungarian paprika is protected and prized for its rich, sweet flavour.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How slowly cooking onions builds natural sweetness, why paprika should never be fried over high heat, and how slow cooking transforms inexpensive cuts of beef into tender, flavourful meals.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A wonderful European comfort food that showcases how a handful of simple ingredients can create remarkable depth of flavour. It also freezes exceptionally well.
chapman-tips: Hungarian sweet paprika is worth buying specifically for this recipe.
Never let paprika fry over high heat or it will become bitter.
The stew tastes even better the following day.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze in family-sized portions for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Excellent reheated gently and served with fresh bread or buttered potatoes.
